text:
There is something cosmically honest about the person who, confronted with the sequential tyranny of the meal, simply reaches for the cake. We have constructed, around the dinner table, an entire moral architecture — the vegetables must precede the pudding, the savory must earn the sweet — as though the universe itself had opinions about macronutrient order, as though the cosmos were watching, clipboard in hand, to ensure no one received pleasure before sufficient suffering. But consider what we are actually doing when we mandate this progression: we are rehearsing, in miniature, the bleakest Protestant theology, the doctrine that joy must be deferred until all obligations are discharged, that sweetness arrives only as reward for the dutiful consumption of whatever is green and lukewarm.

The dessert-first heretic understands something profound about mortality that the vegetable-first moralist has suppressed. You do not know, with any certainty, that you will make it to the crème brûlée. Life interrupts. The phone rings. Someone chokes on a breadstick. The restaurant catches fire — improbable, yes, but precisely as improbable as the assumption that tomorrow is guaranteed, which assumption underpins every sensible retirement plan ever devised. The person who eats dessert first is simply an honest actuarian of pleasure, pricing in the volatility of existence.

And there is this, finally, which no one mentions: the appetite is sharpest at the beginning. You arrive at a meal with the full force of your wanting intact, uncomplicated by bread, by soup, by the long negotiation of a main course. To direct that magnificent, undiminished hunger toward a tiramisu is not indulgence. It is the most perfect allocation of a finite and precious resource.
